1: Psalm 137 is the most explicitly exilic text in the Psalter — it describes the experience of being in Babylon in historical present tense, almost certainly written during or immediately after the Babylonian captivity (587-539 BCE). "By the rivers of Babylon" — the two great rivers of Mesopotamia (Tigris and Euphrates) with their extensive canal systems made Babylon a water-rich landscape dramatically different from Judah's hills. Jewish exiles were settled in communities along these waterways (Ezek 1:1-3 — Ezekiel was by the Chebar river; Ezra 8:15 — exiles gathered by a canal). "We sat and wept (gam-bākînû)" — the weeping was ritualized mourning — perhaps at the river because ritual washing required water (cf. Ezek 1:1 — Ezekiel's visions came by the river).
2: "There on the poplars (ʿărābîm) we hung our harps" — the traditional translation "willows" is likely incorrect; the trees along the Euphrates and its canals were ʿărābîm (Euphrates poplars, Populus euphratica), which grow in rows along Mesopotamian waterways. Hanging the harps (kinnôrôt) on the trees was a symbolic act of musical suspension — the instruments silenced because Zion's worship could not continue in a foreign land. The harp was the primary instrument of the Levitical singers (1 Chr 25; 2 Chr 29:25). To hang up the harp was to stop being what they were: the singers of YHWH's praises.
3: "For there our captors asked us for songs, our tormentors demanded songs of joy (śimḥāh); they said, 'Sing us one of the songs of Zion!'" — the request for performance entertainment from captors was a form of humiliation. The "songs of Zion (šîrê ṣiyyôn)" were the temple hymns — liturgical music tied to specific places, times, and purposes in the Jerusalem cult. To perform them as entertainment for captors was to desacralize them. The same "songs of Zion" are celebrated in Ps 87:7 and Pss 46, 48, 76, 84 — pilgrim songs tied to the physical experience of Zion. The exiles' refusal to perform them as entertainment was an act of liturgical integrity.
4: "How can we sing the songs of the LORD while in a foreign land (ʾereṣ nēḵār)?" — the theological crisis of exile: the songs of YHWH were constitutively connected to the place of YHWH's presence. This was not merely cultural attachment but a profound theological question about whether YHWH's presence was portable. Deuteronomy had tied worship to "the place the LORD your God will choose" (Deut 12:5, 11, 14). Ezekiel would answer the question by having YHWH's kābôd depart from the temple before Babylon's destruction (Ezek 10-11) and then follow the exiles to Babylon — YHWH was present there too. But in this psalm, the theological problem is still raw: how does worship function without the sanctuary?
5: "If I forget you, Jerusalem, may my right hand forget its skill (tiškāḥ yĕmînî)" — the self-curse sworn by the professional musician: may my playing hand forget how to play. The harp player's right hand is his professional identity. The syntax in Hebrew is elliptical ("may my right hand forget") — the implied object is the skill/function of the hand. Some modern translations smooth this to "may my right hand wither," but the Hebrew simply omits the object, leaving the curse hauntingly incomplete. Jerusalem as the object of memory — the very city whose songs they refused to perform for entertainment — is what must never be forgotten.
6: "May my tongue cling to the roof of my mouth (tiḏbaq-lĕšônî lĕḥikkî) if I do not remember you, if I do not consider Jerusalem my highest joy (ʾal rōʾš śimḥātî)" — the second self-curse: may my voice fail if I fail to remember Jerusalem. The tongue-clinging-to-palate image appears in Lam 4:4 (the infants' tongues cleave to the roof of their mouths for thirst — the siege condition). The phrase "highest joy (ʾal rōʾš śimḥātî)" — literally "at the head of my joy," the apex of all happiness. Jerusalem is not merely important but the organizing center of all joy for the psalmist. The professional singer whose highest joy should be music now places city above craft.
7: "Remember, LORD, what the Edomites did on the day Jerusalem fell. 'Tear it down,' they cried, 'tear it down to its foundations!'" — Edom's involvement in Jerusalem's destruction is documented in Obad 10-14 (the entire book condemns Edom for "standing aloof" and actively participating in Jerusalem's looting), Lam 4:21-22, and Ezek 25:12-14. Edom was Israel's closest kinship neighbor (descendants of Esau/Jacob's twin — Gen 36) — their participation in the sack was experienced as betrayal by a brother nation. The cry "tear it down (ʿārû ʿārû)" — strip it bare, raze it — was directed against the temple mount itself, demanding total destruction.
8: "Daughter Babylon, doomed to destruction (bat bābel hašĕḏûḏāh), happy is the one who repays you for what you have done to us" — the personification of Babylon as "daughter (bat)" follows the prophetic convention of personifying cities as women (Isa 47:1-15 — "Virgin Daughter Babylon"; Isa 37:22 — "Virgin Daughter Zion"). "Doomed to destruction (hašĕḏûḏāh)" — already-destined for destruction, a prophetic perfect (the destruction is so certain it is spoken of as already complete). This confidence was grounded in Isaiah's oracles (Isa 13-14; 47) against Babylon. The imprecation that follows is harsh — but its logic is lex talionis: what you did to us will happen to you.
9: "Happy is the one who seizes your infants and dashes them against the rocks" — the most disturbing verse in the Psalter, and one of the hardest passages in Scripture. Context essential: (1) This is lex talionis expressed as a beatitude — Babylon killed Judean infants during the sack of Jerusalem (Lam 2:20-21: "women have eaten their offspring... see, O LORD, those whom you have slaughtered... young men and young women"; Lam 4:4 — infants clinging to mothers dying). (2) The imprecation calls for what Babylon did to be done to Babylon — a precise mirror judgment. (3) Isa 13:16-18 prophesied exactly this for Babylon ("Their infants will be dashed to pieces before their eyes"). The psalm is not instruction but lament-prayer expressing the full horror of the exiles' trauma and their reliance on divine justice. It is preserved in the canon as honest testimony to the depths of human suffering and the legitimacy of bringing even this pain to God.
